data.title: HP Inc.: Cheap On Paper, Challenged In Reality
data.description:
I buy into quality companies when they're on a massive sale and hold them for the long term. Buying into fear is a double boost to total returns. We're profiting from a high dividend yield that can be reinvested into other bargains, and over the long term, our portfolio is benefiting from multiple expansion. If done right, we're outperforming the market, achieving staggering total returns, with companies that others would deem "boring" at best.

After doing this kind of investing for many years, the best way to describe it is compound interest on steroids with below-average risks. It's a lonely road, but a profitable one.

I always start my analysis by screening for companies that are trading at low P/FCF ratios. Preferably in the single digits, but I'm willing to pay up if the company has low debt levels or is of great quality. Out of the thousands of companies that my screener spits out, I may only invest in a handful. AI isn't going to replace due diligence anytime soon when it comes to value investing with a focus on quality.

In my view, quality does not necessarily mean high growth, but consistency and predictability. When it comes to free cash flow generation, we don't want negative surprises. This way, we have a higher chance of avoiding costly value traps.

HPQ is Cheap on Any Valuation Metric

HP Inc. (HPQ) is a company that has been showing up on my screeners lately. Trading at a 6.24x P/FCF ratio and a 7.46x P/E, HP is exceptionally cheap. Despite this, the company's profitability over the last decade has been stable. EPS and dividends grew because of substantial share buybacks. It's a shareholder-friendly company that distributes most of its FCF to shareholders through dividends and buybacks.

In my view, it's a classic Benjamin Graham bet. If you hold a diversified portfolio of companies with the same risk/reward profile as HP at current valuations, you're going to do well over the long term. Some of them will turn out to be value traps, but the winners will more than make up for the losses.

For my own concentrated portfolio, however, HPQ is a pass even at the current low valuations. I'd revisit my thesis if shares were to decline even further from here. The lower the valuation, the lower the risks and the higher the expected total return. Since I don't hold HPQ myself, I won't issue a "Buy" rating and will rate the shares a "Hold."

The reason behind my cautiousness, despite the stable cash flows over the last decade, is simple. The company's high-margin printer business is in a slow secular decline. The PC business is growing over the cycles, but it's a cyclical and commoditized low-margin business. HP is mostly competing on price in this segment. There is some brand equity, as the brand is widely known, but most customers wouldn't pay a premium for an HP computer if the competition were to offer a better deal.

With its limited pricing power, not only does lower demand disproportionately hurt profitability, but higher costs also eat into margins. The prices for memory have spiked. As a business with low margins, that's bad news, and it's already showing in sharply lower profitability in the PC segment this fiscal year.

Selling PCs is highly cyclical. Demand tends to depend on the economy and on refresh cycles. Even the market leader, Lenovo, has low gross and net margins, erratic cash flows, and a stock price that has gone nowhere for decades.

It's a tough business, and as a long-term investor, I'm not a fan of holding companies with below-average profitability unless they're screaming value. HPQ isn't quite there yet.

HP: A Story of Printers and Computers

If I had to describe HP's business model in one sentence, I'd say it sells printers and computers. The printing segment is the high-margin cash cow, but it's declining year after year.

If it weren't for the secular decline, the printing segment would be a business I'd love. You give away the devices for a low price and charge high margins on the ink and toner cartridges. It's like the highly profitable razor and blade model.

The issue is that printing is in a secular decline, and HP faces competition from low-cost third-party providers selling ink and toner cartridges. Offices and people simply print out less than they used to, and it shows in the numbers.

Net revenues in the printing segment declined from $18 billion to $16.7 billion for a CAGR of -3.75% from 2023 to 2025. Margins held up well, but I'd be cautious about expecting them to hold up forever. Printer unit volumes have been down for years. If you're selling substantially fewer printers, that obviously will hurt demand for cartridges, and it will weigh on margins eventually.

Year: 2025 2024 2023 2022 2021
Printer Unit Volume: -4.2% -7.2% -10.2% -12% +3.0%

As a long-term investor, I also look at long-term trends. With a cyclical company like HP, we're gaining a better understanding of how the business is performing over different cycles.

Since its full year as a standalone company in 2016, HPQ's operating profit in the printing segment has been flat. It had $3.1 billion in operating profit in 2016 and $3.1 billion in 2025. Margins improved from 17.1% to 18.7%.

If unit volumes keep declining — and there's no reason to believe that this long-term trend will reverse — at one point, margins will decline as well. So, the printing segment is endangered. The profits from the PC segment will have to make up for it, but it's not a quality business by any means.

The Computer Segment

The computer segment, or the Personal Systems (PS) segment as HP calls it, has shown growing revenues but declining margins over the last three years.

Earnings from operations were essentially flat from 2023 to 2025. Over the last decade, operating profits in the segment almost doubled from $1.15 billion in 2016 to $2.05 billion in 2025 for a CAGR of 6.63%.

The unit volumes look more promising than the printing segment, at least in recent years, but overall, even the computer unit sales aren't in high-growth mode by any means.

Year: 2025 2024 2023 2022 2021
PS Unit Volume: +4.3% +3.3% -14.5% -16.9% +9.8%

And we shouldn't forget, the printing segment remains the core driver of profitability, and it's caught in a serious secular decline. The computer segment is a cyclical and low-margin business. Overall, HP's net margin of 4.86% in 2025 is low. So, altogether, I'd describe HPQ as a low-margin cash cow with long-term challenges.

A margin collapse in the printing segment could quickly turn HPQ into a value trap because the main drivers of total returns are the dividends and share buybacks. It's the main risk for shareholders, despite its rock-bottom valuation. A rapid margin collapse is almost never priced in, not even at a 7x P/E.

Free Cash Flow and Shareholder Yield

I might not be enthusiastic about the overall quality of HP's underlying business, but any serious analysis should always include the valuation. At 7x earnings and a 6% dividend yield, the shares are cheap. At least some of the risks have been priced in.

Despite the flattish operating profits over the last decade, EPS and FCF/share are up sharply since 2016, mostly because of generous buybacks.

Earnings have been in a free fall since 2023, but that's likely just the cyclical nature of the industry and demand normalization after COVID. Lenovo, the market leader, also saw a sharp downturn in profitability during the same period.

As long as HP can generate healthy amounts of free cash flow to fund the dividends and buybacks, the shares are offering value at current levels. So, a "Sell" rating would be overly harsh, but the quality of the underlying business also doesn't justify a "Buy" rating at current valuations. A neutral "Hold" rating seems appropriate given the risk/reward profile from here.

Conclusion

HP isn't an exceptionally high-quality business but a low-margin cash cow with some long-term challenges. Given its risk profile, the current valuation isn't as convincing as it might appear at first glance.

For now, I'll let this one pass. I'll keep it on my watchlist until the valuation reflects a screaming bargain that fully compensates for the risks involved in the secularly declining printing business.

It's a lonely road, but skipping the riskier deals is how we reduce risks and enhance returns. That said, HPQ could have a place in a well-diversified portfolio if you're less risk-averse than I am. And in my experience, many investors out there are.

data.title: Hilton Worldwide Holdings: Solid Growth But At A Full Valuation
data.description:
Shares of Hilton Worldwide (HLT) have been an excellent performer over the past year, gaining about 50%. The company has benefited from ongoing strength in travel spending among higher-income consumers, which has offset some pressure at the lower end. Its significant organic growth pipeline and fee revenue streams have also helped boost growth. That said, its Q1 results were solid but not perfect, sending shares a bit lower in early trading. I last covered Hilton in February, rating the stock a “Hold” as strong fundamentals offset its full valuation in my view. Since then, shares are basically flat, consistent with the rating. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit HLT.

Q1 results were solid

In the company’s first quarter, Hilton earned $2.01 per share, which beat estimates by $0.04. While revenue was up 9% to $2.9 billion, this was slightly below consensus, given some softening in the Middle East. Revenue per available room (RevPAR) is one of the most important metrics in the hotel sector, speaking to the demand and efficiency of its operation; it grew a very solid 3.6% in the quarter. Hilton also continues to return capital to shareholders and boosted its outlook.

Hilton generated $901 million of EBITDA in Q1. This was up 13% from last year thanks to ongoing margin expansion as it builds scale. Franchise fees jumped $71 million, or 11%, to $696 million, reflecting the continued growth in its system. Incentive fees grew a relatively modest 5% to $76 million, likely reflecting some signs of softening in travel demand, which I will discuss further below. Hotels it owns grew revenue 6% to $249 million while their expenses fell by $4 million to $235 million, contributing to much of the margin expansion.

Pipeline remains very impressive

During the quarter, Hilton added 11,000 net rooms, and its system is 6.3% larger than a year ago. It also added another 26,000 rooms to its pipeline, which has reached 527,000. Even as it continues to bring new hotels online, its backlog has grown 5% from last year. These rooms under development provide a clear backlog of future revenue growth. With ~1.35 million rooms currently in service, this pipeline represents ~37% cumulative growth. As such, we should see HLT sustain 6+% annual room growth at least through 2030.

Systemwide, occupancy was up 1.4% to 67.4%, led by strength in Asia and Europe. Unsurprisingly, the Middle East fell 4%. Undoubtedly, the outbreak of the Iran war during the quarter hurt travel in the region. I expect this decline to worsen in Q2, as there was just 1 month of impact in Q1. Even with a ceasefire, I expect international business travel to the region to run significantly lower for some time. Importantly, this region is only 4% of the business, so any decline has a modest overall impact.

While Hilton is largely a U.S. business today, I would reiterate that its pipeline is tilted more overseas. Here, the Middle East is 10%, or ~2.5x its current business weight. Asia is nearly 4x as large at 34%. Over time, this will make Hilton a more globally diverse business. These markets are likely to face more pressure from geopolitical uncertainty and higher oil prices in 2026, though with this pipeline being multiple years in nature, I do not believe this adds meaningful risk to the pipeline.

Growth was balanced across brands

As noted above, the system grew RevPAR by 3.6%. In Q4, we saw a clear delineation with higher-end hotels outperforming its more value-oriented brands. Growth was more evenly distributed in Q1. Tru, its lowest-cost brand, still increased RevPAR by 2.6%, while the Waldorf Astoria grew just 0.8%. Mid-tier brands, like Curio, Canopy, and Tapestry, led the way, each growing over 6%. This balanced growth was actually encouraging for me to see. The “K-shaped” economy is an ongoing concern, especially in discretionary categories like travel, but that fear did not materialize in Q1.

Still, I am cautious on travel spending among lower- and middle-income households this year. We are still in the infancy of this energy price shock, especially as most trips are booked weeks or months in advance. We are likely to see fuel costs lead to higher airfare and squeeze household budgets to an extent, which may lead consumers to pull back on some discretionary activity. Especially with financial markets so robust, higher-income consumers are likely to be insulated from this shock, but that may not be true further down the income spectrum. As such, I expect this divergence between brands to reappear, especially during the summer vacation season.

The balance sheet supports capital returns

Hilton maintains a reasonable balance sheet with $12.5 billion of debt; importantly, it has no material maturities within the next year. That leaves leverage at just about 3.1x, a healthy place to run given its recurring cash flows. With its cash flow, Hilton continues to aggressively return capital to investors. It pays just a token 0.2% dividend, and instead, it focuses on buybacks. In Q1, it repurchased $860 million of stock. Thanks to these buybacks, its share count is down about 4.5% from a year ago. Hilton is on track to meet my ~$3.3 billion buyback expectation this year.

Alongside results, Hilton increased its EPS guidance range by $0.14 to $8.79-$8.91 while continuing to project 2%-3% RevPAR growth and net unit growth of 6%-7%. When looking at guidance, I reiterate the fact that HLT’s outlook does not include the benefit of future share buybacks, which is a reason it tends to run structurally below consensus and increases during the year (consensus is currently $9.05). The benefit of ongoing buybacks should be worth at least $0.22, bridging the gap between HLT’s view and consensus.

With a full valuation, shares are just a "Hold"

Back in February, I was targeting $9.10-$9.30 in EPS. While Q1 was in line with my view, I do see incremental risk for the rest of the year from the energy market dislocations, especially on overseas travel. As such, I am now looking for $9.05-$9.20 in EPS. Given its reliance on franchise fees, Hilton is less exposed to a modest softening in travel demand, and I do not view current macro volatility as changing the long-term tailwind from its pipeline.

Similar to when I last covered Hilton, it scores very poorly on Seeking Alpha’s valuation screener, getting an “F” rating. Shares trade at a substantial premium to the consumer discretionary sector, though its forward P/E trades at only a modest premium to its own history. In other words, much of its relative premium is structural in nature, reflecting its clear organic growth pipeline and franchise fees. I expect these tailwinds to continue to support its valuation. That said, given how elevated valuation already is and potential cyclical pressures on travel, I struggle to see the case for further multiple expansion from here.

Shares are trading at about 36x, which is by no means cheap. I continue to see the company having ~13% EPS growth through at least 2031, reflecting 6%-7% room growth, 3%-4% share count reduction, and modest operating leverage. With EPS doubling in about 5.5 years, Hilton can steadily grow into its multiple and generate market-like performance. At this multiple, though, I believe the outperformance has largely occurred, especially with a mixed near-term outlook for travel demand. As such, I remain a “Hold.” With a supportive buyback and backlog, there is no rush to sell, but I believe investors should wait for shares to pull back to $300 before buying.

data.title: Ventas: Senior Housing Growth Appears Priced In
data.description:
Introduction

So far in 2026, Ventas (VTR) has managed to marginally outperform U.S. REIT peers held in the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ), building on the strong returns achieved in 2025.

While solid Q1 2026 results and an increased 2026 outlook have provided a near-term tailwind for the shares, my overall rating remains a Hold. My neutral investment outlook is justified by:

  • VTR trades at 21.9x its expected 2026 normalized FFO, reduced to 20x if we account for further occupancy gains and associated margin improvements.
  • VTR employs modest leverage, providing near-term safety against unexpected downturns, but likely resulting in a drag on long-term shareholder returns.
  • VTR's U.S. occupancy has already increased in 2024-2026, indicating that a key driver of normalized FFO growth may peter out in the next few years.

Q1 2026 Results Overview

VTR delivered normalized FFO of $0.94/share, up 9.3% Y/Y, benefiting from a larger portfolio as well as a 8.7% Y/Y increase in same-store cash net operating income [NOI].

NOI growth was again driven by gains in the senior housing operating portfolio [SHOP], where same-store segment NOI surged 15.4% Y/Y, with SHOP NOI now accounting for 58% of Ventas's overall Q1 2026 NOI. NOI growth for the remainder of the portfolio was much more modest, at around 2% Y/Y.

The outsized increase in SHOP NOI was partially driven by a 3.7% occupancy improvement in the U.S. SHOP portfolio, reaching 87% in Q1 2026. Even so, U.S. occupancy remains below the 97.2% level observed in Canada, indicating that Ventas could potentially see another few years of above-average NOI growth, assuming U.S. occupancy continues to grow.

I should note that SHOP growth is not coming from occupancy gains alone. Revenue per occupied room [RevPOR] increased 5% Y/Y, reflecting strong pricing power.

To sum up, the only red flag I would highlight with regard to VTR's Q1 2026 results is that performance is increasingly being driven by developments in the SHOP segment, amplifying risks if operating conditions deteriorate. With this caveat in mind, let's discuss the REIT's marginally increased 2026 outlook.

Higher 2026 Outlook

Alongside its Q1 2026 results, VTR also increased its full-year normalized FFO outlook to $3.82-3.89/share, or $3.86/share at its midpoint, up 0.8% relative to previous expectations. The improved guidance reflects marginally increased occupancy expectations, as well as higher SHOP investment volumes of $3 billion, up $0.5 billion relative to initial expectations.

The company ended Q1 2026 with net debt of only $12.76 billion, indicating that net debt only accounts for 23% of the $55.5 billion enterprise value. As such, the change in market pricing for Fed rate cuts from 2026 to 2027 has had a limited impact on VTR's 2026 financial outlook.

All in all, we see that VTR is taking advantage of growth opportunities in the SHOP segment to increase investments, conservatively funding its growth with equity capital, as reflected in a 504 million expected share count for 2026, notably higher than the 486.7 million shares outstanding at the end of Q1 2026.

Valuation and Rating

After a strong start to 2026, VTR trades at 21.9x its 2026 normalized FFO, arguably a quite elevated level for a commercial REIT. The silver lining is that the normalized FFO is achieved with a modest leverage level. At the same time, strong SHOP performance should continue to drive above-average normalized FFO growth for at least a few more years.

Indeed with NOI growth running at 2% outside of the SHOP segment (i,e. in line with inflation and not a major differentiator relative to other REIT peers), the million dollar question with regard to VTR clearly remains for how long can the company sustain its SHOP momentum.

I think increasing occupancy remains a low hanging fruit for VTR. If we assume that occupancy will ultimately rise in the high 90s percent, we see that the company can reasonably deliver three more years of 3% annual occupancy gains (against the current level of 87% occupancy for the U.S. portfolio). The good news is that higher occupancy also increases margins (the cash NOI margin jumped 1.4% to 29.1% in Q1 2026).

I estimate that higher margins alone could boost normalized FFO by about 1%. U.S. occupancy gains of about 9% in total, increasing occupancy to the high 90s percent (roughly 3% annually spread across several years), could likewise lift normalized FFO by about 8%. As such, my calculations show that VTR's normalized FFO should grow by approximately 9% in excess of what other REITs can deliver, benefiting from strong momentum in the SHOP segment.

The normalized FFO growth can be even higher if VTR can sustain above-inflation RevPOR increases, although I see such increases as unsustainable in the long term. Likewise issuing shares at the elevated 21.9x normalized FFO multiple to fund growth opportunities could boost normalized FFO growth. As the company's size grows however, incremental investments have a diminishing impact on overall returns.

To sum up, I estimate that VTR can reasonably reach a normalized FFO of about $4.20/share (i.e. 9% higher than current levels) over the next few years even before factoring in price increases of about 2% annually. I think approaching VTR's valuation from this viewpoint allows investors to gauge the company's idiosyncratic growth profile, as opposed to growth coming from general economic factors such as 2% inflation which are common for all U.S. REITs (notwithstanding somewhat higher inflation in 2026 due to high energy prices).

Even after accounting for this outsized growth however, we see that the normalized FFO multiple remains quite elevated, at about 20.2x. As such, I think that the market has already priced in VTR's significant growth pipeline, confirming my previous Hold rating on the shares.

Risks

In light of increased NOI exposure to the SHOP segment, a deterioration in operating performance in senior housing arguably remains the principal risk in the VTR investment case. While the company is keen to point out tailwinds such as an ageing U.S. population and limited new supply hitting the market, it is also worth highlighting that recent RevPOR increases may make senior housing unaffordable. This in turn may result in VTR's occupancy stabilizing around current levels, even as the overall total addressable market (broadly defined as U.S. citizens aged 80 and over) continues to grow.

I would also highlight that VTR's leverage is quite low, at only 23% of enterprise value. While keeping a low debt level helps during unexpected downturns and adverse financing conditions, it also acts as a drag on returns during favorable periods. As such, I would argue that the company's low leverage is at odds with the robust operating performance in recent years. In essence, VTR seems to be preparing for a downturn that may never come, hurting long-term shareholder returns in the process.

Last but not least, as with any REIT that seeks to attract investors with its dividend distributions (VTR only resumed dividend growth in 2025 after keeping the dividend unchanged in the aftermath of the COVID-19 pandemic), a sustained increase in long-term interest rates may reduce the relative attractiveness of VTR relative to other quasi fixed-income alternatives (I see REITs as closely resembling an inflation linked bond, with payments potentially growing with inflation and construction costs if a REIT can maintain pricing power).

Conclusion

With U.S. markets close to all-time highs, finding real value opportunities among large cap stocks is quite hard. While I do see a path to VTR outperforming the S&P 500 over the long term (largely driven by high current S&P 500 valuations), the path for VTR to outperform REIT peers is arguably quite narrow, especially after recent occupancy gains in 2024-2026.

I should note that being sceptical on VTR has not been a winning strategy, as the REIT's premium valuation has kept expanding even after my very first Hold rating in 2024. Back then U.S. occupancy stood at 81.7%, indicating that roughly 40% of the one-off occupancy gain is behind us (assuming VTR eventually reaches a U.S. occupancy rate on par with its Canadian portfolio, which is far from certain). Even so, the normalized FFO multiple has jumped from 19x in August 2024 to 21.9x in April 2026.

While I agree that the continued rise in occupancy and associated margin improvements will easily lead to VTR reaching a normalized FFO of $4.20/share (before inflation driven growth, which is arguably achievable for most REITs, hence not a real catalyst in my opinion), this is more than reflected in the current share price, hence warranting a Hold rating on VTR.

For investors who plan on holding their VTR shares even after the recent rally, I see high SHOP exposure, low leverage, and a potential rise in long-term rates as principal risks in the investment case.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Verizon Q1: Promising Customer Growth, Shares Attractive
data.description:
Verizon’s (VZ) new CEO, Dan Schulman, had his work cut out for him when he took the reigns of VZ in late 2025.

Coming into the role, the mobile carrier was losing core customer wireless customers, and it was falling further behind T-Mobile (TMUS) following its merger with Sprint.

Sure enough, in the first full quarter under Schulman’s leadership, VZ outperformed expectations. The company added net postpaid phone connections for the first time since 2023, and it also raised its full-year profit guidance.

Improving VZ’s customer growth and reigning in costs were two big intentions of Schulman stepping into the CEO role. It’s perhaps fortunate timing that this fell into place for him in Q1.

Granted, one can’t exactly say VZ’s Q1 story was solely attributable to Schulman’s leadership. After all, it was his first quarter in the pilot’s seat. VZ’s quarterly results, nonetheless, were promising, and I believe the prospects of a continued turnaround could be a key catalyst for VZ moving forward.

I am bullish on shares of VZ, and I believe long-term investors could be rewarded with both attractive share price upside, as well as steady recurring dividend income.

VZ Stock Key Metrics

Based on its most recent quarter, VZ is growing total revenues at about 3% YOY. And within this topline is mobility and broadband service revenue, which grew 1.6% in Q1. It’s worth noting that the 1.6% growth was despite an 80-basis-point hit from customer credits tied to the January network outage.

From a total customers standpoint, VZ ended Q1 with 146.8M total wireless retail connections. In addition, during Q1, VZ also closed on its +$9.6B acquisition of Frontier Communications. I expect this to further support the base of connections in the periods ahead.

I also believe the closure of the deal expands VZ’s fiber footprint and strengthens its ability to bundle wireless and home internet, a strategy VZ has repeatedly emphasized as central to improving customer lifetime value and reducing churn over time.

Despite this improving profile, VZ still trades at just 9.5x forward earnings and about 5.3x forward cash flow.

I view both as steep discounts to sector medians. With a nearly 6% dividend yield and Wall Street seeing roughly 10% upside, I view the valuation as attractive, especially in-line with VZ's improving fundamentals.

VZ Q1 Results Recap

As mentioned in my stock metrics section, topline revenues grew 2.9% YOY, while mobility and broadband service revenue increased 1.6% despite an 80-basis-point headwind from customer credits related to the January outage.

On the earnings call, VZ mentioned that Q1 should represent the low point for service revenue growth this year. This was based on trends showing improvements as the quarter progressed. This should bode well for the forward outlook, in my view.

While it may seem as if VZ’s Q1 was mixed based on the news ticker showing a beat on earnings and a miss on revenues, I view the quarter as a ‘beat’ overall.

My view that the quarter was a ‘beat’ stems from the subscriber momentum figure. Here, VZ posted 55K phone net adds, the first positive Q1 result since 2013. In addition, it was a 344K improvement versus last year.

Broadband was also strong with 341K net adds, including both fixed wireless and fiber. Just as important, churn improved throughout the quarter, with consumer postpaid phone churn exiting March below 0.85%. At the same time, acquisition and retention costs fell roughly 35%.

This in turn drove much healthier unit economics. Those better customer economics flowed directly into profitability, with adjusted EPS rising 7.6% YOY and adjusted EBITDA margins expanding 140 basis points.

What Is The Outlook For VZ Stock?

I view the outlook ahead as positive, and I attribute this in part to the raised forward guidance that was provided on the earnings call.

VZ now expects adjusted EPS growth of 5% to 6% for 2026, up from the prior 4% to 5% range. Additionally, postpaid phone net adds are projected to land in the upper half of the 750K to 1M range for the year.

Meanwhile, mobility and broadband service revenue growth guidance of 2% to 3% was reaffirmed, with Q1 described as the low point as trends improved throughout the quarter.

Free cash flow (“FCF”) is expected to grow roughly 7% or more this year, supported by lower acquisition costs, improving churn, and operating expense savings as part of the company’s +$5B cost reduction program. VZ also expects to end the year with more than 32M fiber passings. I view these as all positive marks for the forward outlook.

Is VZ Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

I view VZ as a 'buy' at current trading levels.

In Q1, VZ grew customers, improved churn, lowered acquisition costs, expanded margins, raised guidance, and generated stronger cash flow all at the same time. That’s not something VZ investors have been able to say in years, especially with regards to customer growth.

These improvements are happening while the company is also reshaping its operating model with its new CEO, integrating Frontier to expand its fiber footprint, and leaning into a strategy that I believe should structurally improve customer lifetime value over time.

Despite the improvements, the stock still trades at less than 10x forward earnings and just over 5x forward cash flow, while paying a dividend yield near 6%.

Consensus targets see roughly 10% upside from here, and that’s before factoring in the potential for multiple expansion if VZ continues to turn around its operations.

As this turnaround further materializes, I believe that investors will continue to be paid attractively with a high yielding and well-covered dividend payout. All considered, for long-term investors I view shares as a ‘buy.’

data.title: Celestica Q1: AI Revenue Soars While The Stock Plunges, I'm Buying
data.description:
Introduction

Celestica Inc. (CLS) looks like it might become one of the major victims of this earnings season. The company has reported an impressive quarter, beating all of the expectations and raising previously expected guidance, yet the stock is down by almost 14% in the premarket as I’m writing this article, wiping out the returns of the previous two weeks.

For the experienced investors, this kind of reaction is not surprising, as stocks like Celestica (trending, rapidly growing, expensive) are widely exposed to the overall market fluctuations. Given the Iran war uncertainties, mixed with fears and excitement before the Mag 7 earnings, additional volatility this week is expected.

At the same time, let’s not forget that Celestica is one of the major winners of 2025. The stock has been consolidating since the beginning of this year until approximately the ceasefire news, but since then, it has been up by almost 65% prior to this report.

I follow the tech sector closely and every industry that rallies on the AI-demand boom, so I view this moment as a great opportunity to start Celestica coverage. Just a month ago, I broke down Micron’s (MU) earnings, giving it a Strong Buy rating, even as the stock was selling off. Even though the momentum after previous gains has been violent, as of today, Micron has outperformed the S&P 500 (SP500) by about 5%, and the reason is simple—the numbers have fully supported the bullish thesis. From the headlines, it looks like Celestica may be in the exact same position, but to make sure, we have to break down the results.

Celestica

Every time I initiate coverage of the company, especially in the tech sector, I like to make sure everyone knows what we are talking about and what business model the company has. It is incredibly important, as without a deep understanding of a company's positioning, it’s easy to ignore important financial metrics and indicators, which make up the whole picture.

If you know this company well, you can skip this part, but if you are not that familiar, I suggest reading it in case you’ll learn something new. According to Seeking Alpha:

Celestica Inc., operates through two segments, Advanced Technology Solutions, and Connectivity and Cloud Solutions. The company offers a range of product manufacturing and related supply chain services, including design and development, new product introduction, engineering services, component sourcing, electronics manufacturing and assembly, testing, mechanical assembly, systems integration, precision machining, logistics, asset management, product licensing, and after-market repair and return services. It also provides hardware platform solutions, which includes development of infrastructure platforms, and hardware and software design solutions and services, including open-source software that can be used as-is or customized for specific applications; and management of program, including design and supply chain, manufacturing, and after-market support, including IT asset disposition and asset management services.

Simply put, Celestica is an electronics manufacturer that creates critical hardware for various industries, but the rapid growth of the stock is being powered by the AI infrastructure buildout. This is the part the market is focused on. Even if you have never heard about Celestica but you were curious about data centers, most likely you’ve seen their products—for example, storage systems, rack integrations, routers, networking switches, including 800G and 1.6T switches, and a lot of other important parts of the modern data center. As the demand for training and inference of AI models grows, the compute demand also grows. The buildout of the capacity would be impossible without companies like Celestica, because there would have been nothing to do with the fastest modern chips, if not for the infrastructure that allows them to operate at full capacity. For this reason, the main customers of Celestica are hyperscalers and some other large industrials.

Even though both Micron and Celestica are seeing fast growth due to the rapid AI adoption, comparing them (and the stock behavior) directly wouldn’t be correct. Micron is operating in the highly cyclical industry where memory is effectively a high-tech commodity, which companies spend a lot of money on when the new development comes in, but then the cycle goes down, and all of the metrics, especially profitability, plunge. This is the reason the market has decided to punish Micron so much after the last earnings—there are fears that the cycle is about to end. Just as the market realized the cycle was still in full swing, the stock started growing again.

With Celestica, it’s not exactly the same. It sells not only a part needed to turn data centers online, for example, switches. Instead, the company is an Electronic Manufacturing Services (EMS) provider, which means that they can design and manufacture a full machine. This machine can be created using some of the parts from other companies. For example, it can be powered by Nvidia (NVDA) chips, using memory from Micron, and even some optics from Marvell (MRVL) or Coherent (COHR). It means that while the geopolitical situation and the overall market demand could impact the business of this company, it shouldn’t enter into a violent cycle swing as Micron does. As long as CapEx spending will be high for hyperscalers, Celestica will be one of the beneficiaries of that spending. Because the company designs and manufactures full solutions, it’s not easy to replace it with a competitor once you’ve started working with this company. At least, not as easy as it is to replace a memory provider.

For investors, it effectively means that if clients of Celestica didn’t start lowering their CapEx or didn’t start lowering their guidance for the spending massively, there isn’t much to worry about in the general landscape. Of course, the execution is also important, but if the company delivers nice results, it’s clear that selloffs like today’s happen only due to the sentiment.

Q1 2026

This quarter has been an absolute success. The company not only beat the adjusted EPS expectations, but it also raised guidance going forward, which essentially confirms that the demand is expected to stay resilient and even grow.

Revenue is in line with the expectations, even though it says “miss,” as a miss of under $1 million on $4.05 billion in sales in a quarter is a meager drop. At the same time, revenue of $4.05 represents a staggering 53% YoY growth.

This revenue growth has been primarily driven by the CCS segments (Connectivity and Cloud Solutions), or the demand from data centers, which I discussed in the previous part of this article. This segment alone has brought over $3 billion, growing by 76%, while ATC (Advanced Technology Solutions) has been flat from the previous year, even slightly dropping.

The company doesn’t have high margins. Even without calculations, we see it is hovering near 10%, although they have slightly expanded from 10.3% a year ago to 10.8% today, bringing about $437 million as a gross profit for the quarter. It means a couple of things: the company has not only managed to sell way more, but it also sells more valuable solutions, which bring higher margins, and it’s good. On the other hand, the company doesn’t benefit from a shortage in the industry, as Micron does in memory, and it doesn’t show any pricing power.

At the same time, after paying for R&D, marketing, general expenses, taxes, and everything else, it got to a net income of $212.3 million, which is an increase of almost 150% from a year ago, meaning the company is scaling organically, without the need to “buy” the demand or to invest heavily in the development upfront, which lowers risks and points to a lighter business model than most of the semiconductor manufacturing peers.

For every share, investors own the company earned $2.16, beating the higher end of the guidance and showing a growth of 80% from a year ago. The company buys back the shares, increasing shareholders' value even further. Cash is down from about $600 million in Q1 2025 to $378 million this quarter, as the company spends money on new property and equipment to expand its manufacturing capacity. The company expands its borrowing power, meaning the investment phase here isn’t over, but at the same time, it is a relatively safe position to be in, as they won’t run out of money soon.

Valuation & Risks

Even though the valuation doesn’t look cheap on most of the traditional metrics, it is, in fact, undervalued by the PEG ratio before accounting for this quarter, meaning it will be even cheaper when the valuation upgrades.

Given a $360 stock price (as I’m writing this) and 115 million shares outstanding, the market cap is currently at about $41 billion. Calculating the trailing and forward price-to-earnings, I got ~50x on TTM, and ~35x on a forward basis. While TTM P/E is way higher than the sector, based on the FWD, the stock is almost in line with the 33x sector median. Before the correction, based on the PEG ratio, which normalizes the whole sector for growth, the company has traded at a major discount to its peers (25%-40%), and after reporting this quarter, the dispersion will only increase. For fast-growing companies in fast-growing industries, this metric makes the most sense, as it normalizes all of the peers and shows accounting for growth how cheap or expensive this stock is.

The fact that the stock has a substantial upside based on the valuation doesn’t mean there are no risks. In fact, there are quite a few. The main problem is the concentration. The growth is entirely driven by the data center buildout, which is being sponsored by a handful of main hyperscalers. Three big customers together account for 65% of the sales of Celestica, meaning if one of them suddenly decided to drop back, the impact on the business would be huge and immediate, leaving little time to react for investors. There are risks related to AI fears, which may not be justified for now, but even the sentiment around it could be enough to drag the stock lower, as we see today. Any global supply chain setbacks would be a negative catalyst as well. Finally, the expensive valuation multiple on traditional metrics could mean higher volatility.

Conclusion

Even though there are some risks for Celestica, the execution and overall market are still on the rise. As long as the CapEx spending remains elevated, the company will be showing rapid growth, and the stock has room to grow, especially after the drop today. I see more opportunities than threats, at least for the near future, in 2026, so I rate this stock as a Buy, and I am planning on opening a short-term position myself.

data.title: Acres Commercial Realty: The High-Yielding Preferred Stock Could Be Interesting
data.description:
Introduction

Acres Commercial Realty (ACR) is a real estate finance company that focuses on commercial real estate. Its primary objective is to invest in first mortgage loans as well as first priority and subordinated interests in other first mortgage loans (with in excess of 80% of its portfolio backed by loans on multifamily properties). Its average commercial real estate loan has a size of between 10 million and 100 million USD. During 2025, the average realized spread was approximately 3.35% over the one month benchmark interest rate, and that spread is how the REIT makes its money.

Acres will report on its first quarter results this week, and I'm keeping an eye on the stock and the company to see if the common shares and/or the preferred stock could be an interesting investment right now.

Managing $2.2B in assets, but planning to expand

The 2025 results offer an interesting look under the hood. I will not dwell on the annual financial results considering the Q12026 results around the corner. But as you can see below, Acres generated total revenue of just under $80M, on which it generated approximately $16.4M in operating income. Keep in mind the company also has some real estate assets on its balance sheet, which means it incurs depreciation and amortization expenses as well. Meanwhile, the total revenue already includes the interest expenses, considering it's an important element of Acres’ business model.

Looking at the bottom line, we see net income of approximately $28M which was sufficient to cover the $21.1M in preferred dividends. This coverage ratio looks disappointingly low, especially when you see net income includes the gain on the sale of real estate assets, but keep in mind this includes in excess of 10 million in non-cash depreciation and amortization expenses.

The common shareholders barely made a profit considering the EPS was just 3 cents.

I'm mainly interested in the company's preferred shares, so I'm somewhat okay with a low preferred dividend coverage ratio as long as the balance sheet is robust.

Looking at that balance sheet, Acres is managing approximately $2.16B in assets, which includes $166M in real estate assets and approximately $86M in cash and cash equivalents.

As you can see in the image above, the total amount of equity is approximately $421M. This includes approximately 8.3M preferred shares for a total of $215M. This means the preferred equity represents approximately half of the total equity attributable to Acres Commercial. That ratio is increasing as Acres has been buying back common shares in the past year as it wants to capitalize on the opportunity on buying back stock at a 30-40% discount to NAV.

While I generally prefer to have a bigger cushion of common equity that ranks junior to the preferred stock, there's more than meets the eye here. The average LTV ratio of the commercial real estate portfolio is approximately 77%, which means the approximately $1.8B in CRE loans are backed by roughly $2.3B in real estate. While the LTV ratios are up for discussion in the current economic climate, the track record of Acres Commercial Realty is pretty good, as it had to record only a small amount of realized losses in the recent past.

On the most recent conference call, the management highlighted that while resolving $368M in troubled loans in the past five years, it only has a record of approximately $4.8M in losses on these loans. This represents just over 1%, which further strengthens the management's track record in dealing with issues.

While this does not mean loan losses will continue to remain very low, this provides some comfort that the average LTV ratios can be relied upon.

What to look out for when it publishes its Q1 financials?

The REIT will publish its financial results for the first quarter of this year later this week, and there are a few elements I'm really looking forward to.

First of all, I want to see if the recent economic climate has pushed additional loans into the riskier categories, and it will be interesting to hear management comments on how it perceives the current situation on the markets. Almost a quarter of the loans will mature this year, and this will allow Acres to recycle the cash into new loans, unless there are other issues popping up.

Secondly, I'm looking forward to seeing how efforts to grow the portfolio are going. While the cost of debt has gone up, the yield on new investments will have moved up as well. Just last month, Acres mentioned it intended to grow its loan book by $500-700M, and in a recent presentation, it outlined what the impact of an expanded portfolio could be.

The torque is pretty obvious, and expanding the loan portfolio to $2.5B would result in $1.52 per share in earnings available for distribution. Going more aggressive and expanding the portfolio to $2.75B could then boost the result to almost $3/share.

I’m mainly interested in the preferred shares, although it's definitely good to see the preferred dividends will see an improved coverage ratio. But it's clear the payout ratio will always remain pretty high.

The preferred shares offer an interesting risk/reward ratio

I have been keeping an eye on the Series C preferred shares (ACR.PR.C) issued by Acres Commercial Realty (no position right now). This is a fix-to-float preferred that came out of the gate with an 8.625% preferred dividend yield, but the preferred dividends are currently floating based on the 3M interest rates (with a mark-up of almost 600 bps).

The past few quarters, the quarterly dividend came in around $0.60 per share, which means the current yield is approximately 9.5-9.7%, as the current share price is exactly $25/share, which is the principal value. The Series C preferred stock is callable at any time, but the downside risk is limited as the stock isn’t trading at a premium to its call price.

Investment thesis

I think the common shares of Acres Commercial Realty could be rated as a "speculative buy," but that’s depending on the REIT effectively expanding its loan book (and thus improving its earnings result). As explained in this article, increasing the loan book will provide a massive tailwind for the earnings attributable to the common equity holders of Acres.

While the preferred dividend coverage ratio isn’t very high, I think the preferred shareholders could still argue the risk/reward ratio is attractive with the 9.6% yielding preferred shares Series C (of course with the caveat that a decreasing 3M SOFR will also cause the quarterly preferred dividends to decrease). The Series D preferred stock has a fixed rate and currently yields 9.15%.

While I’d certainly classify the Acres Commercial Realty preferred shares in the more speculative category. I currently have no position in any of the securities issued by Acres Commercial Realty, as I may wait for the Q1 results before deciding on a long position and the size of a potential initial position.

data.title: BRC: Coffee Price Stabilization And Stronger Retail Distribution (Upgrade)
data.description:
As we head into the thick of the Q1 earnings season, like many investors, I'm wondering how to diversify my portfolio away from near-all-time highs in the S&P 500. The large-cap semiconductor trade is becoming incredibly crowded, which means we have to look toward more small- and mid-cap plays to find true value.

Black Rifle Coffee Inc., or BRC (BRCC), looks like a more promising bet. Coffee prices are beginning to soften, which has been a major overhang on the company's profit growth. On top of this, Black Rifle Coffee is expanding its wholesale distribution network at just the right time, with rivals potentially fading. BRC is up slightly this year, but has lost ~50% of its value over the past year.

I last wrote a "Sell" opinion on BRC in November, when the stock was trading at ~$1.30 per share. Make no mistake: I think risks still abound in this name. That said, I do think last year's selloff has taken a lot of downside out of this name, while fundamentals may be looking up with coffee prices softening. I'm upgrading BRC to a "Neutral" rating.

The next major catalyst for BRC will be its Q1 earnings release, due on May 5. While I don't necessarily think the stock is a safe buy pre-earnings, this is now a solid name to add to your watch list.

Coffee prices are softening amid a conservative margin guidance target for FY26

Let's now discuss the primary factor that has moved in BRC's favor since last quarter and which may benefit the company as it heads into earnings season. Over the past year, BRC's gross margin profile has been under sharp scrutiny as weather events took a chunk of supply in Vietnam and Brazil offline. Now, however, we're seeing signs of normalization in the market.

The chart below, taken from BRC's latest investor presentation dated as of early March, denotes the company's latest expectations and thinking around gross margin. In FY25, the company suffered a sharp ~6 point contraction in gross margins to 34.6% due primarily to coffee inflation, with Q4 clocking in even worse at a 32.1% margin.

That said, the company still sees a pathway to normalizing back to a 40%+ long-term gross margin, of which 1 point of benefit owes to an expectation of softening coffee prices.

Note that BRC laid out these expectations as of the end of February, as noted in the chart above, when futures prices were trading around ~$2.80-$2.85 per pound of Arabica coffee for future delivery.

We note that this stabilization trend has continued across March and April. As shown in the chart below, coffee futures for July 2026 delivery are currently changing hands at ~$2.86.

On a rolling settlement basis, coffee futures are down ~20% over the past 3 months, ~25% over the past 6 months, and ~28% over the past year. And yet when we consider this, we think BRC's guidance for FY26 may be understated.

As shown in the chart above, BRC is currently guiding to 34%-36% gross margins for FY26, the midpoint of which is essentially flat to 34.6% margins in FY25. As listed above, the company is expecting positive margin drivers from price increases, favorable sales mix, and higher production volumes, but baked into this estimate is an expectation of input cost inflation plus tariffs. While we certainly don't argue with the fact that a full-year impact from tariffs will be a headwind, we think this outlook certainly doesn't fully capture the benefit of sliding coffee prices this year. And given the stabilization in future prices below the $3 mark in March and April, we think it's likely for BRC to raise its full-year gross margin expectations alongside its Q1 earnings update.

Stronger distribution network

The other point that needs to be made is that BRC is making healthy progress in wholesale. We appreciate the fact that the company has deprioritized its DTC sales channel as well as its company-owned cafes (which it calls Outposts). Wholesale distribution is now the core focus of the business; wholesale revenue in FY25 grew 5% y/y to $258 million and represented 65% of overall revenue.

The underlying story that may not be getting enough attention is that though it's a small brand, BRC is penetrating into a very crowded category and taking share from incumbents. The chart below showcases that BRC's packaged coffee retail sales achieved 22% unit growth last year, while brands like Starbucks (SBUX) and Dunkin' declined.

The company also has further upside in FY26 from a full-year benefit of its brand-new energy drink category. Black Rifle Energy has expanded to an impressive ~19k distribution doors, including with a major national retail partner in Walmart (WMT).

We think that the tough macroeconomy and, in particular, high gas prices may help to shift more consumer spending away from in-store coffee/drink consumption and to home consumption. My view is that brands like Dutch Bros (BROS) that rely heavily on drive-through orders will take a hit in comp sales growth owing to recent gas price pressures, and that this spending will shift favorably into packaged at-home products that BRC is focusing on.

Valuation and key takeaways

Even against what I view to be a conservative guidance forecast, BRC trades quite cheaply. At current share prices of $1.15, BRC trades at a market cap of $286.5 million. Netting off the $4.3 million of cash and $34.7 million of debt on BRC's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $316.9 million.

For FY26, BRC's revenue expectations of "at least 7% growth" translate to $426.2 million in revenue. Its expectations of "at least 30% adjusted EBITDA margins" result in $127.9 million in adjusted EBITDA. Bear in mind, as previously noted, that this EBITDA margin outlook is based on a 34%-36% gross margin, which may be understated if coffee prices continue to stabilize lower.

Even against these conservative estimates, BRC trades at 2.5x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA. This is cheap, but of course we should consider the risks. The retail environment is becoming increasingly promotional, and BRC may need to invest more in trade discounts to keep growing its wholesale channel. Coffee prices could also return on an upward trajectory.

With all of these factors in mind, I'm still on the sidelines for BRC ahead of its May 5 earnings date, but with most catalysts tilting positive, we expect the stock to be prepared for a broader rebound soon.

data.title: GMR Solutions Targets IPO On High Debt, Contracting Revenue
data.description:
GMR Solutions’ Revenue Is Contracting, Debt Is High

GMR Solutions Inc. (GMRS) has filed to raise capital via an IPO of its Class A common stock, according to an S-1 registration statement filed recently.

GMR provides emergency transportation and related medical services throughout the United States.

The company's top line revenue has fallen due to divestitures, but debt remains high amid a “higher for longer” interest rate environment, and the balance sheet has been effectively loaded up with high debt versus assets, which is generally typical in a PE-sponsored IPO.

Although my initial outlook is negative, I’ll provide a final opinion when we get more information from management.

What Does GMR Solutions Do?

GMR provides emergency medical services for on-site care and transportation in approximately 1,400 counties in the U.S.

The firm serves around 15,000 patients per day via its team of over 24,000 clinicians, who responded to ~10% of all 911 calls and 37% of emergent air medical requests in 2025.

GMR’s non-emergency services consist of non-emergency patient care services.

In 2025, its emergent care accounted for 84% of patient encounters, while non-emergent care accounted for 16%.

The company's revenue contribution by type of revenue for 2025 is shown in the pie chart below:

The firm is led by Chairman and Chief Executive Officer Nick Loporcaro, who has been with GMR since 2023 and was previously CEO of Landmark Health, a healthcare provider to chronically ill patients in the U.S. He was also President of McKesson’s U.S. Pharma and Specialty Health businesses.

Investors in the company include private equity firm KKR (KKR), Koch Stockholder and private equity firm HPS.

Together, they have invested fair market value of $901.6 million as of the end of 2025.

What is GMR’s Market?

GMR operates primarily in the U.S. emergency medical services [EMS] market. The global EMS market was an estimated $36.7 billion in 2025 and was forecast to exceed $66 billion by 2033, according to a market research report by S & S Insider.

The report states that the industry is expected to grow at a CAGR of nearly 7.7% from 2026 to 2033, a moderately solid rate of growth.

The market will likely see increased demand from the aging U.S. population requiring greater emergency medical services and increasing urbanization, leading to a higher incidence of road traffic accidents and other emergency situations.

The U.S. EMS market was over $9.8 billion in 2025 and was expected to exceed $17.4 billion by 2033, representing a CAGR of 7.5% from 2026 to 2033.

The chart below shows the U.S. EMS market’s historical and projected future growth trajectory through 2033:

Competitors that GMR faces include the following companies:

  • Air Methods

  • Metro Aviation

  • PHI Air Medical

  • Life Flight Network

  • Falck

  • Priority Ambulance

  • Acadian Ambulance Service

  • Local service providers.

Recent industry trends include the growing adoption of GenAI-based triage technologies that improve operational efficiencies for EMS providers.

The company's markets are highly competitive in that they compete not only with other companies providing services, but also with local fire departments, hospitals, local governments and volunteer entities.

The firm’s strengths are its large-scale EMS platform, diversified service model and critical demand profile of its clients.

Weaknesses include high labor intensity, difficulty in finding and retaining qualified personnel, heavy dependence on payer reimbursement, and public-sector win-lose contracting.

Its opportunities are to expand its EMS contracts, grow its interfacility and specialty transportation services, improve its fleet utilization through technology, and consolidation.

Threats include wage inflation, reimbursement cuts, liability cost volatility and local competition.

The company is also active in the non-emergency medical services market, although that revenue currently is only about 16% of total revenue.

GMR’s Recent Financial Results

Revenue fell year-over-year in 2025, although operating income rose, as did net income and cash flow from operations, as the table of recent financial results shows here:

Total Revenue

Period

Total Revenue

% Variance vs. Prior

Year Ended Dec. 31, 2025

Operating Profit (Loss)

Period

Operating Profit (Loss)

Operating Margin

Year Ended Dec. 31, 2025

$ 751,596,000

Year Ended Dec. 31, 2024

$ 616,206,000

Net Income (Loss)

Net Income (Loss)

Year Ended Dec. 31, 2025

$ 206,227,000

Cash Flow From (Used in) Operations

Year Ended Dec. 31, 2025

$ 641,146,000

Year Ended Dec. 31, 2024
